summary: I have been working here in Montana for many years, currently as the administrator at the Discovery Care Center Support. Montana is where my long-term care facility is located, and I’ve been serving this community for the past 14 years. It’s a less densely populated area compared to urban centers, which influenced how COVID-19 gradually affected us here. Places like Hamilton and Reveille County in Montana have been part of my daily experience managing healthcare during the pandemic.

I am the administrator at Discovery Care Center Support, a long-term care facility in Montana that provides assisted living and skilled nursing services. I’ve been in this role for 14 years, overseeing daily operations and ensuring the safety and well-being of our residents. During the COVID-19 pandemic, we had to implement many new policies like visitor restrictions and PPE stockpiling to protect our vulnerable population. It’s been challenging but rewarding to support both staff and residents through these times.

The COVID-19 pandemic started impacting our facility around March 2020, and we quickly had to adapt to new safety protocols. We followed guidance from CMS and the CDC to implement visitor restrictions, staff masking, and stockpiling PPE and food supplies. It was a gradual arrival in Reveille County and Hamilton, Montana, but the emotional toll on residents, especially those with dementia, was significant. The pandemic reminded me of past health crises like the HIV/AIDS epidemic and severe influenza seasons, but this was on a much larger scale.

entity: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
summary: Throughout the COVID-19 pandemic, I relied heavily on the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention for guidance and recommendations. The CDC issued numerous documents that helped shape our facility’s policies on masking, visitation, and infection control. However, the volume and sometimes contradictory nature of their guidance made it challenging to interpret and apply in real-time. Still, their role was critical in helping us protect our residents and staff during this unprecedented event.
cues: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

summary: The Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services played a big role in issuing QSO documents that guided our facility’s response to COVID-19. These documents, sometimes over 150 pages with multiple revisions, detailed infection control requirements and visitation rules specific to nursing homes like ours. It was often difficult to keep up with the volume and changes, but CMS’s guidance was essential for compliance and resident safety. We had to carefully track which documents applied to our facility and adjust policies accordingly throughout 2020 and 2021.

Reveille County, Montana, is part of the community where our facility is located, and it experienced a gradual onset of COVID-19 cases starting around April or May 2020. Because it’s a less densely populated area, the virus didn’t hit all at once, but we still had to implement strict visitor restrictions and staff precautions early on. Watching the situation evolve in Reveille County helped us prepare and respond thoughtfully to protect our residents and staff.

Hamilton, Montana, is another community near our facility where COVID-19 began to impact daily life around spring 2020. Like Reveille County, Hamilton saw a gradual increase in cases, prompting us to enforce visitor limitations and enhanced safety protocols. The local context of Hamilton shaped how we managed the pandemic response, balancing caution with the needs of our residents and staff.

Having worked in healthcare for over 42 years, I remember the HIV/AIDS epidemic in the early 1980s as a significant health crisis. When COVID-19 emerged, it reminded me of that time in terms of the uncertainty and fear among healthcare workers and patients. Both events required rapid adaptation and heightened infection control measures, though COVID-19’s scale and impact were even more widespread. The HIV/AIDS epidemic shaped my perspective on managing infectious diseases in vulnerable populations.

Throughout my nursing career, I’ve seen many severe influenza seasons that prepared me somewhat for the challenges of COVID-19. Influenza outbreaks taught me the importance of infection control and vaccination efforts in long-term care settings. When COVID-19 arrived, it felt like a more severe version of those bad flu seasons, requiring even more stringent precautions. These past experiences helped inform our facility’s response to protect residents from respiratory illnesses.

While I didn’t mention the Montana Health Care Association directly in our conversation, it’s an important organization that supports facilities like ours across the state. They provide resources and advocacy for long-term care providers in Montana, which has been valuable during the pandemic. Their guidance and networking opportunities help administrators like me stay informed and connected with best practices statewide.
